text.fullText: February 17, 2026 Submitted Via Regulations.gov Jessica Kramer, Assistant Administrator Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Re: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Dear Assistant Administrator Kramer: Great Lakes Indian Fish and Wildlife Commission (GLIFWC or Commission) staff submit the following comments on the proposal to alter requirements for water quality certification under Clean Water Act Section 401. The Commission is a natural resource agency exercising delegated authority from 11 federally recognized Indian tribes in Michigan, Minnesota, and Wisconsin.1 These tribes retain reserved hunting, fishing and gathering rights in territories ceded to the United States in various treaties (see map), rights that have been reaffirmed by federal courts, including the US Supreme Court. 2 The ceded 1 GLIFWC member tribes are: in Wisconsin – the Bad River Band of the Lake Superior Tribe of Chippewa Indians, Lac du Flambeau Band of Lake Superior Chippewa Indians, Lac Courte Oreilles Band of Lake Superior Chippewa Indians, St. Croix Chippewa Indians of Wisconsin, Sokaogon Chippewa Community of the Mole Lake Band, and Red Cliff Band of Lake Superior Chippewa Indians; in Minnesota – Fond du Lac Chippewa Tribe, and Mille Lacs Band of Chippewa Indians; and in Michigan – Bay Mills Indian Community, Keweenaw Bay Indian Community, and Lac Vieux Desert Band of Lake Superior Chippewa Indians. 2 Among others, see: Lac Courte Oreilles v. Voigt, 700 F. 2d 341 (7th Cir. 1983), cert. denied 464 U.S. 805 (1983); Lac Courte Oreilles v. State of Wisconsin, 775 F. Supp. 321 (W.D. Wis. 1991); Fond du Lac v. Carlson, Case No. 5-92-159 (D. Minn. March 18, 1996) (unpublished opinion); Minnesota v. Mille Lacs Band of Chippewa Indians, 119 S. Ct. 1187 (1999); United States v. State of Michigan, 471 F. Supp. 192 Docket No. EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 February 17, 2026 Page 2 territories extend over portions of Minnesota, Wisconsin and Michigan and include portions of Lakes Superior, Michigan and Huron. GLIFWC’s focus is off-reservation treaty rights, and it is from that perspective that these comments are submitted. GLIFWC staff’s comments on this rule should not be construed as precluding comments by individual member tribes from their own sovereign and on-reservation perspectives. GLIFWC member tribes reserved their ceded territory treaty rights in order to guarantee that they could continue their hunting, fishing, and gathering way of life (or “lifeway”) in a manner that meets their subsistence, economic, cultural, medicinal, and spiritual needs. The full exercise of this lifeway requires access to clean, healthy and abundant natural resources, which require clean water to thrive. The federal government’s obligations as a treaty signatory, therefore, require it to provide water resources with the greatest protection possible. To do less would undermine the fulfillment of US treaty guarantees. GLIFWC’s member tribes understand that clean water is fundamental to life. They regard it as “the first medicine” and as the blood of their mother, the earth. With this perspective in mind, it would be difficult to overstate the importance of nibi (water) to the spiritual, cultural, medicinal and subsistence practices that underlie the tribal lifeway. GLIFWC's member tribes also believe that actions affecting natural resources must be judged on how well they will protect seven generations hence. They seek to ensure that principles of ecosystem management and biological diversity recognize and protect the fundamental interdependence of all parts of the environment. GLIFWC’s governing Board of Commissioners (Board) consistently supports laws and policies that provide for the protection and restoration of water resources. The Board also supports tribal assertions of regulatory authority over reservation lands and waters, including assumption of various programs under the Clean Water Act. A number of GLIFWC’s member tribes have assumed “treatment as a state” status under the Clean Water Act, have adopted water quality standards, and issue certifications pursuant to Section 401. These water quality standards cannot be violated by upstream dischargers, providing protection for off-reservation as well as on-reservation water resources. GLIFWC staff submitted extensive comments on the 2023 Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule. That rule strengthened cooperative federalism in the implementation of the Clean Water Act and strengthened the ability of tribes to protect the quality of water on their lands. The current rule proposal, in contrast, fundamentally undermines tribes’ (and states’) ability to protect water quality through the 401 certification process. In addition, the proposed rule appears to be a solution in search of a problem. EPA provides no evidence that there are problems associated with the current administration of section 401; it appears that these changes will be the cause of inefficiencies rather than a solution to them. The proposed rule undercuts the notion that states and tribes are in the best position to know what is necessary for the protection of water quality. It requires action in the face of incomplete or poor information, with no ability to request that an applicant withdraw and resubmit a certification request. It severely restricts the scope of conditions by requiring only an assessment of the discharge, not the (W.D. Mich. 1979); United States v. State of Michigan, 520 F. Supp. 207 (W.D. Mich. 1981). Docket No. EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 February 17, 2026 Page 3 activity as a whole. Furthermore, it requires agreement by the federal government and the applicant for both modifications and their specific contents, and undermines settled procedures governing neighboring jurisdictions. These provisions are ill-advised, poor policy, and should be rejected entirely. In addition to the fact that the proposed rule is poor policy, some provisions appear to be contrary to the Clean Water Act and to Supreme Court precedent. For example, the Supreme Court ruled in PUD No. 1 of Jefferson County and City of Tacoma v. Washington Department of Ecology, 511 U.S. 700 (1994) (PUD No. 1), that the activity triggering a federal permit is the appropriate focus of a 401 certification, not solely the discharge. No subsequent cases, including Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369 (2024), support the EPA’s argument that it may now reinterpret section 401 despite the holding in PUD No. 1. It is Congress’ role to change the law if it disagrees with the Supreme Court’s reasonable and settled interpretation, not an agency’s in a rulemaking process. The proposed rule severely restricts states’ and tribes’ ability to impose conditions that will ensure compliance with other “appropriate requirements” of state or tribal law. That existing language provides ample authority for the EPA to defer to those other governments in determining what requirements are appropriately considered when protecting that state’s or tribe’s water resources. The rule’s proposal to restrict conditions disregards the “appropriate requirements” language and belies its purported deference to state and tribal authority. In addition, EPA should not limit “water quality requirements” to only numeric water quality criteria. Narrative standards are often used by tribes to protect cultural uses of water that may not easily be translated into numeric criteria, but which are nevertheless crucial parts of a tribe’s standards. The proposed rule further undermines its purported attempts to standardize and clarify the contents of a request by narrowing the information required, and by limiting certifying agencies’ ability to gather information from an applicant. This, combined with the limited information needed to trigger the “reasonable period of time (not to exceed one year)” within which an agency must decide, will result in an increase in denials, and/or force decisions based on incomplete information. The proposed rule should retain the existing language of sections 121.5 and 121.6. The proposed rule must not undermine 401 certifications by eliminating them where there is no “applicant,” e.g. in nationwide and regional general dredge and fill permits. There is no statutory basis or other authority for EPA to make this change, and there is no other way for states and tribes to ensure that these types of federal projects do not impact water quality. The current definition of “neighboring jurisdiction” is appropriate and should not be changed. The current proposal would add confusion to an important provision that protects on-reservation water quality. In addition, limiting the notification of the neighboring state or tribe solely to the narrow definition of discharge is inconsistent with section 401 and the overall purpose of the Clean Water Act. Finally, there is no need for a deadline within which a public hearing on a neighboring jurisdiction’s objection must be held. The 2023 rule created flexible treatment-as-a state (TAS) options for tribes that enabled them to have more authority over water quality on their reservations. Staff capacity is an ongoing issue on tribal reservations, and the 2023 rule allowed tribes to obtain TAS for section 401 without the additional burden of applying for and implementing a section 303(c) water quality standards program. The 2023 Docket No. EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 February 17, 2026 Page 4 rule also allowed tribes to obtain TAS for the sole purpose of participating as a neighboring jurisdiction under section 401(a)(2) without taking on the responsibility of a section 401 water quality certification program. EPA appropriately justified the creation of these separate TAS programs in recognition that not all tribes have the necessary, dedicated resources to apply for and administer the more costly and time- intensive TAS programs. These accommodations are appropriate and should be retained. From an off-reservation perspective, tribes that reserved rights to hunt, fish, and gather within ceded territories are not often the regulatory authority with control over enforcement of water quality standards. The tribes rely on other governments to exercise their authorities in ways that preserve and enhance the water, air, and habitats that support healthy and abundant natural resources. This rule diminishes the ability of 401 certification authorities to impose conditions that will protect water quality from activities requiring a federal permit, thereby compromising the natural resources that are the subject of tribal treaty rights. The federal government’s treaty and trust obligations require more. I have asked Ann McCammon Soltis of my staff to be available to answer any questions you may have, she can be reached at 715-685-2102 or amsoltis@glifwc.org. Thank you for your consideration of these comments. Sincerely, Jason Schlender Manidoo Noodin Executive Administrator
